description: The provided image is an irrelevant placeholder icon showing a blue-yellow circular logo, red bookmark symbol, and the text "Check for updates", no valid content related to the target material science research is visible. The corresponding study focuses on three Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) austenitic low-density steels with varying Mn/Ni ratios, processed by 50% cold rolling followed by annealing at 873-1273K, investigating microstructural evolution (recrystallization, κ-carbide and B2 phase precipitation behavior) and mechanical properties (hardness, tensile strength, elongation). However, no specific figure content such as microstructure images, diffraction patterns, or performance curves can be identified from the current visual data, all material related information is derived from the provided paper text extraction rather than the image itself.

This figure presents EBSD characterization results for three Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) austenitic low-density steels (28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, 15Mn5Ni) under hot rolling plus 50% thickness reduction cold rolling (HR+CR) condition. The first column (a,d,g) are inverse pole figure (IPF) maps of FCC austenite, the second column (b,e,h) are image quality (IQ) maps, and the third column (c,f,i) are phase contrast maps (red for FCC austenite, blue for B2 ordered phase) with a 20 μm scale bar. 28Mn0Ni shows single FCC phase, with the blue arrow in (b) marking slip bands formed during cold rolling. 28Mn5Ni contains a small fraction of dispersed B2 precipitates. 15Mn5Ni has abundant band-shaped B2 phase, and the inset in (g) shows shearing and fragmentation of B2 phase during cold rolling indicated by yellow arrows. The results demonstrate that reduced Mn content and Ni addition promote B2 phase formation, and B2 phase undergoes fragmentation while FCC matrix forms slip bands during cold rolling, supporting the regulating effect of composition on second phase content and cold rolling deformation behavior.

This figure presents X-ray diffraction patterns of two Fe-Mn-Al-C based austenitic low-density steels under different processing conditions: subplot (a) corresponds to 28Mn0Ni (Ni-free, 28 wt% Mn) alloy, subplot (b) corresponds to 15Mn5Ni (5 wt% Ni, 15 wt% Mn) alloy. Each alloy is tested under three conditions: hot rolling plus 50% cold rolling (HR+CR), HR+CR followed by 1 h annealing at 973 K then water quenching, and HR+CR followed by 1 h annealing at 1173 K then water quenching. Identified phases include face-centered cubic γ austenite, ordered B2 phase, and κ-carbide. Only γ phase is detected in the HR+CR state for both alloys. After 973 K annealing, κ-carbide precipitates in both alloys, while B2 phase peaks appear only in 15Mn5Ni alloy. After 1173 K annealing, κ-carbide fully dissolves in both alloys, while B2 phase remains stable in 15Mn5Ni alloy but is absent in 28Mn0Ni. These results confirm that Ni addition combined with lower Mn content promotes B2 phase formation and its high-temperature stability.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) characterization results of three Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) austenitic low-density steels (28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, 15Mn5Ni) processed by hot rolling (HR), cold rolling (CR), and subsequent 1023 K annealing. Subplots (a), (d), (g) are inverse pole figure (IPF) maps for the austenite phase, with a 2 μm high-magnification IPF inset in (d) revealing fine recrystallized austenite grains in 28Mn5Ni. Subplots (b), (e), (h) are phase contrast maps, where red denotes FCC austenite and blue denotes B2 intermetallic phase. Subplots (c), (f), (i) are kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps characterizing local lattice strain, with a color scale ranging from 0 (min) to 5 (max). 28Mn0Ni exhibits fully recrystallized equiaxed austenite grains with very limited B2 precipitates, while 28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni retain deformed microstructural features with much higher B2 phase fractions and higher KAM values indicating larger residual strain. The results support that reduced Mn content and Ni addition promote B2 phase formation and suppress austenite recrystallization during post-cold rolling annealing.

This figure presents EBSD characterization results of three Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) austenitic low-density steels (28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, 15Mn5Ni) subjected to hot rolling (HR), cold rolling (CR), and subsequent annealing at 973 K or 1173 K. Subfigures (a), (c), (e) are inverse pole figure (IPF) maps of austenite for 28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, 15Mn5Ni after HR+CR+973 K treatment, respectively, while (b), (d), (f) are corresponding austenite IPF maps after HR+CR+1173 K treatment, with an inset in (f) showing IPF map of B2 phase alone revealing its grain recovery. Subfigures (g), (h) are phase maps of 15Mn5Ni after 973 K and 1173 K annealing, respectively, where blue represents B2 phase and red represents FCC austenite, with a 20 μm scale bar for all micrographs. It is observed that as annealing temperature rises from 973 K to 1173 K, austenite recrystallization fraction and grain size increase, while B2 phase dissolves partially. B2 phase is more stable in lower-Mn higher-Ni 15Mn5Ni alloy, and B2-free 28Mn0Ni shows more significant austenite grain growth at 1173 K, confirming that B2 precipitates suppress austenite grain boundary migration, and two-phase region annealing enables grain refinement of low-density steels.

This set of four subplots quantifies the microstructural evolution of three Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) austenitic low-density steels (28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, 15Mn5Ni) after cold rolling followed by heat treatment at 973 K, ~1023 K, and 1173 K. Subplot (a) shows the recrystallized austenite fraction, (b) presents the grain size of recrystallized austenite (unit: μm), (c) displays the twin fraction, and (d) illustrates the volume fraction of secondary B2 phase, with dashed lines in (d) marking the B2 fraction of hot-rolled reference samples for 28Mn5Ni and 15Mn5Ni. All three alloys show increasing recrystallized austenite fraction, recrystallized grain size, and twin fraction as heat treatment temperature rises. The Ni-free 28Mn0Ni alloy exhibits the highest recrystallization rate and largest grain size at each temperature, while Ni-containing alloys (especially 15Mn5Ni with higher initial B2 content) show suppressed recrystallization, smaller grains, lower twin fraction, and higher retained B2 phase fraction at equivalent temperatures, with B2 fraction decreasing as temperature increases for Ni-containing alloys. This figure confirms that Ni addition and higher B2 phase content retard austenite recrystallization and grain growth during post-cold-rolling annealing.

This figure presents TEM characterizations of three Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) austenitic low-density steels (28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, 15Mn5Ni) after hot rolling, 50% cold rolling, and subsequent 973 K annealing for 1 h followed by water quenching. Subfigures (a,c,e,g) are bright-field TEM images, while (b,d,f,h) are dark-field TEM images captured from the (010) κ-carbide satellite spot in selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns, with insets of a and c showing SAED patterns under [101] zone axis of austenite grains. Solid white arrows mark intergranular B2 particles, broken yellow arrows mark intragranular B2 particles (size 5-200 nm), and dotted orange arrows mark κ-carbides (size 10-60 nm). Finer uniformly distributed κ-carbides exist in recrystallized austenite regions, while coarser inhomogeneous κ-carbides are observed in deformed austenite regions. Ni-containing alloys show both intergranular and intragranular B2 precipitates, while Ni-free 28Mn0Ni only presents intergranular B2. The results confirm that strained deformed regions are preferential nucleation sites for B2 and κ-carbide precipitates, and Mn/Ni content regulates the precipitation behavior of secondary phases under 973 K annealing.

This set of 6 bright-field transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images (all with a 2 μm scale bar) presents the microstructures of three Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) based austenitic low-density steels after hot rolling, 50% cold rolling, and 1 h heat treatment at 1173 K followed by water quenching. Subfigures (a,b) correspond to the Ni-free 28Mn0Ni alloy, showing single-phase recrystallized austenite (γ) grains without any secondary phase precipitates. Subfigures (c,d) correspond to the 28Mn5Ni alloy, and (e,f) correspond to the 15Mn5Ni alloy; both Ni-containing alloys exhibit B2 intermetallic precipitates (marked by arrows) distributed in the austenite matrix. The fraction of intragranular B2 precipitates decreases as the recrystallized austenite fraction increases, verifying that highly strained deformed regions are preferential nucleation sites for the B2 phase. This figure supports that Ni addition is essential for B2 phase precipitation at 1173 K, and Mn content also regulates B2 precipitation behavior in this alloy system.

This figure presents the Vickers hardness (VHN) variation of three Fe-Mn-Al-C based austenitic low-density steels, namely 28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, and 15Mn5Ni, under different processing conditions. Subplot (a) plots hardness as a function of cold rolling reduction in thickness (0 to 50%): hardness of all three alloys increases with rising cold rolling reduction, with the hardness order 15Mn5Ni > 28Mn5Ni > 28Mn0Ni at the same reduction, reaching ~460 VHN, ~450 VHN, ~430 VHN respectively at 50% reduction. Subplot (b) shows hardness of 50% cold rolled samples as a function of subsequent heat treatment temperature (873 K to 1273 K): hardness decreases monotonically with increasing heat treatment temperature for all alloys, with the same hardness order as in the cold rolled state across all tested temperatures. The observed trends confirm that cold work hardening, Ni addition, and higher B2 phase content increase hardness, while recrystallization and precipitate dissolution at elevated heat treatment temperatures reduce hardness, supporting the correlation between microstructural evolution and mechanical performance of the tested steels.

This figure presents tensile mechanical properties of three Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) austenitic low-density steels (28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, 15Mn5Ni) under different processing conditions. Subplot (a) shows engineering stress-engineering strain curves for samples treated by hot rolling plus cold rolling (HR+CR), where 15Mn5Ni exhibits the highest ultimate tensile strength (~1800 MPa) but the lowest elongation (~7%). Subplot (b) displays engineering stress-engineering strain curves for HR+CR samples after further heat treatment at 973 K, 1023 K, or 1173 K, revealing that 28Mn0Ni heat treated at 1173 K achieves the maximum elongation of ~86%. Subplot (c) gives true stress-true strain curves of selected heat-treated samples, showing continuous work hardening for all tested alloys. Subplot (d) is the ln(σ) versus ln(ε) plot derived from true stress-strain data to calculate strain hardening exponent n: n of 28Mn0Ni increases from 0.16 to 0.5 as heat treatment temperature rises from 973 K to 1173 K, while the two Ni-containing alloys show an identical n of ~0.3 at 1173 K, indicating higher strain hardening ability of Ni-free 28Mn0Ni alloy. This figure demonstrates that alloy composition and heat treatment temperature significantly regulate the tensile performance and strain hardening behavior of the studied low-density steels.

This figure presents fractographic and microstructural characterizations of three Fe-Mn-Al-C-(Ni) austenitic low-density steels processed by hot rolling (HR), cold rolling (CR), and subsequent 1023 K annealing. Subfigures (a), (b), (c) are SEM fractographs of failed tensile specimens of 28Mn0Ni, 28Mn5Ni, 15Mn5Ni alloys respectively: 28Mn0Ni and 28Mn5Ni show typical dimple morphology corresponding to ductile fracture, while 15Mn5Ni exhibits quasi-cleavage fracture features with abundant microcracks indicating brittle failure. Subfigure (d) is low-magnification SEM image of the cross-section of failed 15Mn5Ni specimen showing intergranular cracks. Subfigures (e) (EBSD inverse pole figure map) and (f) (phase distribution map with red for FCC austenite and blue for B2 phase) confirm cracks propagate along γ/B2 interphase boundaries, revealing intergranular B2 precipitates induce brittle fracture of 15Mn5Ni under this treatment condition.

This set of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images shows post-tensile deformed microstructures of the 28Mn0Ni Ni-free Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel alloy. Subfigure (a) corresponds to the sample processed via hot rolling (HR), cold rolling (CR) and annealing at 973 K, deformed to a true strain ε~0.14, which exhibits dislocation multiplication and dislocation cell formation. Subfigures (b) to (e) correspond to samples processed via HR+CR and annealing at 1173 K, deformed to true strains of ~0.02 (b), ~0.08 (c) and ~0.30 (d, e) respectively. Subfigures (b) to (d) reveal progressive slip band refinement with increasing applied strain, while subfigure (e) shows interaction between dislocations and pre-existing annealing twins. All subfigures have a 200 nm scale bar. The figure supports the conclusion that the observed microstructural evolution processes act as the core strain hardening mechanisms for the alloy.

This set of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images shows post-tensile deformation microstructures of 15Mn5Ni (Fe-15Mn-9Al-0.9C-5Ni) austenitic low-density steel processed via hot rolling (HR), cold rolling (CR), and 1173 K annealing followed by water quenching. Subfigure (a) corresponds to the sample deformed to true strain ε~0.02, revealing the interaction between moving dislocations and B2 intermetallic precipitates. Subfigures (b) (ε~0.05), (c) (ε~0.14) and (d) display the evolution of deformation substructures with increasing strain, specifically showing progressive dislocation multiplication and slip band refinement, with a unified scale bar of 200 nm for all subfigures. The figure verifies that B2 precipitates act as non-shearable obstacles to impede dislocation motion, and the continuous evolution of dislocation configurations and slip band refinement with rising strain is a key contributor to the strain hardening behavior of the 15Mn5Ni alloy under the studied processing condition.
